instruction: A React application has poor Core Web Vitals scores — LCP of 4.2s, CLS of 0.35, and INP of 380ms. Diagnose the likely causes and implement fixes for each metric.
output:
Let me analyze each Core Web Vital and reason about likely causes and fixes. LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) = 4.2s (poor, should be <2.5s): The largest visible element takes too long to render. Common causes: (1) hero image not preloaded/optimized, (2) render-blocking CSS/JS in the critical path, (3) server response time (TTFB) too slow, (4) client-side rendering — the browser downloads JS, parses it, then fetches data, then renders. Fix: preload LCP image, use SSR or static generation for above-the-fold content, optimize server response. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) = 0.35 (poor, should be <0.1): Elements shift after initial render. Common causes: (1) images without explicit dimensions, (2) dynamic content injected above existing content (ads, banners), (3) fonts causing FOUT/FOIT, (4) lazy-loaded components that push content down. Fix: set explicit width/height on images, reserve space for dynamic content, use font-display: swap with size-adjust. INP (Interaction to Next Paint) = 380ms (poor, should be <200ms): User interactions take too long to produce visual feedback. Common causes: (1) heavy JavaScript blocking the main thread, (2) synchronous operations in event handlers, (3) expensive re-renders triggered by state updates, (4) large DOM. Fix: break up long tasks, use requestIdleCallback/startTransition, memoize expensive components. I'll provide concrete code fixes for each. ```jsx // === FIX 1: LCP — Preload hero image + priority loading === // In of your HTML or Next.js _document // // React component with priority image loading function HeroSection({ imageUrl, title }) { return (

{title}

{/* fetchPriority tells browser this is the LCP element */}
); } // For SSR/SSG: move data fetching to server to eliminate client waterfall // Before (bad): useEffect → fetch → setState → render (4 round trips) // After (good): getServerSideProps → render with data (1 round trip) export async function getServerSideProps() { const data = await fetchHeroData(); // runs on server return { props: { heroData: data } }; // HTML includes content } // === FIX 2: CLS — Reserve space for dynamic content === function AdSlot({ width = 728, height = 90 }) { const [adLoaded, setAdLoaded] = useState(false); return ( // min-height reserves space BEFORE ad loads — prevents shift
{adLoaded ? : }
); } // Font loading without layout shift // In CSS: const fontCSS = ` @font-face { font-family: 'CustomFont'; src: url('/fonts/custom.woff2') format('woff2'); font-display: swap; /* show fallback immediately */ size-adjust: 105%; /* match fallback metrics to reduce shift */ ascent-override: 95%; descent-override: 22%; line-gap-override: 0%; } `; // Image component that prevents CLS function StableImage({ src, alt, width, height, ...props }) { return ( // aspect-ratio container prevents shift even before image loads
); } // === FIX 3: INP — Break up long tasks + optimize re-renders === import { startTransition, useMemo, useCallback, memo } from 'react'; // Before (bad): filtering 10k items on every keystroke blocks main thread function SearchBad({ items }) { const [query, setQuery] = useState(''); // This re-renders the ENTIRE list on every keystroke const filtered = items.filter(i => i.name.includes(query)); return ( <> setQuery(e.target.value)} /> ); } // After (good): defer expensive update, memoize list function SearchFixed({ items }) { const [query, setQuery] = useState(''); const [deferredQuery, setDeferredQuery] = useState(''); const handleChange = useCallback((e) => { const value = e.target.value; setQuery(value); // immediate: updates input (feels responsive) startTransition(() => { setDeferredQuery(value); // deferred: React can interrupt this }); }, []); const filtered = useMemo( () => items.filter(i => i.name.includes(deferredQuery)), [items, deferredQuery] ); return ( <> ); } const MemoizedItemList = memo(function ItemList({ items }) { return (
    {items.map(item => (
  • {item.name}
    • ))}
); }); // Break up long synchronous tasks using scheduler yield async function processLargeDataset(items) { const CHUNK_SIZE = 100; const results = []; for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i += CHUNK_SIZE) { const chunk = items.slice(i, i + CHUNK_SIZE); results.push(...chunk.map(expensiveTransform)); // Yield to main thread every chunk so interactions stay responsive if (i + CHUNK_SIZE < items.length) { await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 0)); } } return results; } ``` Summary of fixes: LCP — preload hero image with `fetchPriority="high"` and move data fetching to server. CLS — explicit dimensions on all images, reserved space for dynamic content with `min-height`, font `size-adjust` to match fallback metrics. INP — `startTransition` for non-urgent updates, `useMemo`/`memo` to prevent unnecessary re-renders, and chunked processing with main-thread yields.

instruction: Implement a JavaScript bundle optimization strategy that reduces a 2.5MB webpack bundle to under 500KB initial load. Show code splitting, tree shaking, and lazy loading techniques.
output:
A 2.5MB initial bundle is terrible for performance. The goal is to get the initial load under 500KB while keeping all functionality accessible. Let me think through the optimization strategy. First, analyze what's in the bundle. Common bloat sources: (1) entire lodash imported instead of individual functions, (2) moment.js with all locales, (3) large UI libraries loaded upfront, (4) no code splitting — every route loaded on initial page load. Strategy: (1) Route-based code splitting — only load the code for the current page. (2) Component-level lazy loading for heavy components (charts, editors). (3) Tree shaking — ensure imports are tree-shakeable (named imports, sideEffects: false). (4) Replace heavy libraries with lighter alternatives (date-fns instead of moment, just the lodash functions you need). (5) Dynamic imports for feature flags. Webpack config: splitChunks to extract vendor code (changes rarely = long cache), minimize with terser, and analyze with webpack-bundle-analyzer to find the big offenders. The mental model: initial load should contain only the shell (nav, router, auth) + the first route. Everything else loads on demand. ```javascript // === webpack.config.js — Optimized === const path = require('path'); const { BundleAnalyzerPlugin } = require('webpack-bundle-analyzer'); const CompressionPlugin = require('compression-webpack-plugin'); module.exports = { entry: './src/index.js', output: { filename: '[name].[contenthash:8].js', chunkFilename: '[name].[contenthash:8].chunk.js', // dynamic chunks get unique names path: path.resolve(__dirname, 'dist'), clean: true, }, optimization: { splitChunks: { chunks: 'all', cacheGroups: { // Vendor chunk: react + core libs (changes rarely = long cache) vendor: { test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/](react|react-dom|react-router)[\\/]/, name: 'vendor', priority: 20, }, // Common chunk: shared between 2+ routes common: { minChunks: 2, priority: 10, reuseExistingChunk: true, }, }, }, // Separate runtime chunk so vendor hash doesn't change on app updates runtimeChunk: 'single', usedExports: true, // tree shaking }, plugins: [ new CompressionPlugin({ algorithm: 'gzip', threshold: 1024 }), // Uncomment to analyze: new BundleAnalyzerPlugin(), ], }; // === Route-based code splitting with React.lazy === import React, { Suspense, lazy } from 'react'; import { BrowserRouter, Routes, Route } from 'react-router-dom'; // Each route is a separate chunk — only downloaded when navigated to const Dashboard = lazy(() => import(/* webpackChunkName: "dashboard" */ './pages/Dashboard')); const Analytics = lazy(() => import(/* webpackChunkName: "analytics" */ './pages/Analytics')); const Settings = lazy(() => import(/* webpackChunkName: "settings" */ './pages/Settings')); const UserAdmin = lazy(() => import(/* webpackChunkName: "admin" */ './pages/UserAdmin')); function LoadingFallback() { return
Loading...
; } export default function App() { return ( {/* Nav + layout — always loaded (small) */} }> } /> } /> } /> } /> ); } // === Component-level lazy loading for heavy widgets === function AnalyticsPage({ data }) { const [showChart, setShowChart] = React.useState(false); // Chart library (~400KB) only loads when user clicks "Show Chart" const Chart = React.useMemo( () => showChart ? lazy(() => import(/* webpackChunkName: "chart" */ './components/HeavyChart')) : null, [showChart] ); return (

Analytics

{Chart && ( Loading chart...
}> )} ); } // === Tree shaking: import only what you use === // BAD: imports entire lodash (~70KB minified) // import _ from 'lodash'; // const sorted = _.sortBy(items, 'name'); // GOOD: imports only sortBy (~2KB) import sortBy from 'lodash/sortBy'; const sorted = sortBy(items, 'name'); // BAD: moment.js with all locales (~300KB) // import moment from 'moment'; // GOOD: date-fns with tree shaking (~6KB for what you use) import { format, parseISO } from 'date-fns'; const formatted = format(parseISO(dateStr), 'MMM d, yyyy'); // === Prefetch likely-next routes for instant navigation === function NavLink({ to, children }) { const prefetch = () => { // Prefetch chunk when user hovers over link if (to === '/analytics') import('./pages/Analytics'); if (to === '/settings') import('./pages/Settings'); }; return ( {children} ); } // === package.json: mark side-effect-free for tree shaking === // { // "sideEffects": ["*.css", "*.scss"], // // Tells webpack: all other imports are pure, safe to tree-shake // } ``` Result breakdown: vendor chunk (~150KB gzipped: React + Router), app shell (~30KB), each route chunk (~50-100KB loaded on demand). Initial load drops from 2.5MB to ~200KB gzipped. The chart library only loads when the user actually needs it. Hover-prefetching makes subsequent navigation feel instant.

instruction: Explain the critical rendering path of a web page and implement optimizations to achieve a sub-1-second First Contentful Paint. Include resource prioritization and render-blocking elimination.
output:
The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps the browser takes from receiving HTML to painting the first pixels. Understanding it is essential for performance optimization. The path: (1) Parse HTML → build DOM, (2) Parse CSS → build CSSOM, (3) Combine DOM + CSSOM → Render Tree, (4) Layout (compute positions), (5) Paint (pixels to screen). CSS is render-blocking by default (step 3 can't happen until step 2 finishes). JavaScript is parser-blocking by default (pauses DOM construction until script executes). To achieve sub-1s FCP: (1) Minimize critical resources — only inline the CSS needed for above-the-fold content. (2) Eliminate parser-blocking JS — use async/defer. (3) Reduce critical path length — fewer round trips before first paint. (4) Resource hints — preconnect to CDNs, preload critical assets, prefetch next-page assets. The key insight: FCP happens as soon as DOM + CSSOM are ready for above-the-fold content. Everything else can load later. So we inline critical CSS, defer everything else, and use resource hints to parallelize network requests. I'll show the HTML structure with optimizations and a Node.js script that extracts critical CSS at build time. ```html

Build faster applications

Ship with confidence using our platform.

``` ```javascript // === Build-time critical CSS extraction (Node.js) === const critical = require('critical'); const fs = require('fs'); async function extractCriticalCSS() { const { html, css } = await critical.generate({ src: 'dist/index.html', css: ['dist/styles/main.css'], width: 1300, height: 900, inline: false, // we'll inline manually for control minify: true, penthouse: { timeout: 30000, }, }); console.log(`Critical CSS: ${css.length} bytes`); if (css.length > 14000) { console.warn('WARNING: Critical CSS > 14KB — may not fit in first TCP packet'); } fs.writeFileSync('dist/critical.css', css); return css; } // === Resource priority hints via HTTP headers (Express middleware) === function earlyHints(req, res, next) { // 103 Early Hints — browser starts fetching before HTML arrives if (req.path === '/') { res.writeEarly({ link: [ '; rel=preload; as=font; crossorigin', '; rel=preload; as=script', '; rel=preload; as=image', ], }); } next(); } // === Performance budget enforcement === const BUDGETS = { criticalCSS: 14_000, // must fit in first TCP round trip (~14KB) initialJS: 150_000, // max JS before interaction totalImages: 500_000, // above-the-fold images ttfb: 200, // server response time in ms }; function checkBudgets(metrics) { const violations = []; for (const [key, limit] of Object.entries(BUDGETS)) { if (metrics[key] > limit) { violations.push(`${key}: ${metrics[key]} > ${limit}`); } } if (violations.length > 0) { console.error('Performance budget violations:', violations); process.exit(1); // fail CI build } } ``` Key principles for sub-1s FCP: inline critical CSS (must fit in ~14KB first TCP packet), defer all scripts, preconnect to third-party origins, preload LCP image, and use async CSS loading for non-critical styles. The 103 Early Hints header lets the browser start fetching resources before the HTML even arrives.

instruction: Implement a lazy loading strategy for a content-heavy page with images, iframes, and heavy components. Handle intersection observer, loading states, and error recovery.
output:
Lazy loading is about deferring resource loading until the user is about to see it. The Intersection Observer API is the modern way to do this — it's performant (no scroll event listeners), battery-friendly, and handles all the edge cases (resize, DOM mutations). Design considerations: (1) How far ahead to load — `rootMargin: '200px'` means start loading 200px before it's visible. This gives the resource time to load before the user scrolls to it. (2) Loading states — show a skeleton/placeholder while loading to prevent CLS. (3) Error recovery — images fail, iframes timeout. Need fallback UI and retry capability. (4) Progressive enhancement — if Intersection Observer isn't available (unlikely in 2026 but still), fall back to loading everything. For images: native `loading="lazy"` works but gives you no control over rootMargin or loading states. Intersection Observer gives full control. For iframes: same approach but more important because iframes are expensive (they create entire browsing contexts). For heavy components: React.lazy + Suspense triggered by intersection. I'll build a reusable `useLazyLoad` hook and components that handle all three cases. ```jsx import React, { useRef, useState, useEffect, useCallback, Suspense, lazy } from 'react'; // === CORE HOOK: Intersection Observer lazy trigger === function useLazyLoad(options = {}) { const { rootMargin = '200px', threshold = 0.01, triggerOnce = true } = options; const ref = useRef(null); const [isIntersecting, setIsIntersecting] = useState(false); useEffect(() => { const element = ref.current; if (!element || (triggerOnce && isIntersecting)) return; const observer = new IntersectionObserver( ([entry]) => { if (entry.isIntersecting) { setIsIntersecting(true); if (triggerOnce) observer.unobserve(element); } }, { rootMargin, threshold } ); observer.observe(element); return () => observer.disconnect(); }, [rootMargin, threshold, triggerOnce, isIntersecting]); return [ref, isIntersecting]; } // === LAZY IMAGE with loading/error states === function LazyImage({ src, alt, width, height, className = '' }) { const [ref, isVisible] = useLazyLoad({ rootMargin: '300px' }); const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle'); // idle | loading | loaded | error const [retries, setRetries] = useState(0); const handleLoad = useCallback(() => setStatus('loaded'), []); const handleError = useCallback(() => { if (retries < 2) { // Retry with cache-bust setRetries(r => r + 1); setStatus('loading'); } else { setStatus('error'); } }, [retries]); const imgSrc = retries > 0 ? `${src}?retry=${retries}` : src; return (
{!isVisible && (
)} {isVisible && status !== 'error' && ( )} {isVisible && status === 'loading' && (
)} {status === 'error' && (
Failed to load image
)}
); } // === LAZY IFRAME (YouTube, maps, etc.) === function LazyIframe({ src, title, width = 560, height = 315 }) { const [ref, isVisible] = useLazyLoad({ rootMargin: '400px' }); const [loaded, setLoaded] = useState(false); return (
{!isVisible && (
